data.title: Ancient Chinese DNA shows gender bias in human sacrifice ritual 4,000 years ago
data.description:
Female sacrifice in China dates back thousands of years earlier than previously believed, a study of ancient DNA has revealed.
Evidence from elite burials at a large Stone Age settlement in northwestern China also suggests the existence of an early hierarchical society, with gender preference relating to the type of sacrificial ritual, according to the Chinese archaeologists and anthropologists behind the study.
Shimao, located in the Yellow River valley in Shaanxi province, is one of the largest prehistoric settlements discovered in China and dates to the late Neolithic period, about 3,800 to 4,300 years ago.
By sequencing more than 100 ancient genomes from Shimao and its satellite sites, the research team found that sacrificial victims in mass burials, which may have served public ritual purposes, were mostly male, whereas attendants buried with dead nobles or elites were overwhelmingly female.
“These findings reveal a predominantly patrilineal descent structure across Shimao communities and possibly sex-specific sacrificial rituals,” the team wrote in a paper published in the peer-reviewed journal Nature on Wednesday.
The large stone-walled site at Shimao borders the northern Loess Plateau and the Ordos Desert, covering about 4 sq km (1.5 square miles). It shows features typical of state-level societies, such as craft production, large fortifications and high social stratification involving abundant human sacrifice.

Archaeologists began excavating the site in 2012, when the origins of its population, social structure, kinship patterns and relations with neighbouring groups remained a mystery.
Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences’ Institute of Vertebrate Palaeontology and Paleoanthropology (IVPP), the Shaanxi Academy of Archaeology and other institutions have since sought to fill this knowledge gap.
Fu Qiaomei, a palaeontologist and researcher at IVPP, and her colleagues chose to analyse ancient genomes from individuals buried at the site and in surrounding settlements.
They analysed DNA data from the remains of 169 individuals among the 207 found at seven archaeological sites across Shaanxi and neighbouring Shanxi province, uncovering details about the regional demographics, cultural practices and social structure of the settlement.
The detailed analysis at Shimao and its surrounding sites revealed two types of practices: mass burials that may have served public ritual purposes and sacrifices accompanying high-status burials in which victims were entombed alongside the dead.
Ancient DNA evidence from mass burials such as those at the city’s eastern gate revealed that nine out of 10 sacrificial victims were men.
However, this sacrificial pattern stood in stark contrast to that associated with elite burials, which mostly indicated female victims. The findings represented a distinct social stratification within Shimao culture.
At Hanjiagedan, a cemetery for nobility located south of the inner enclosure, nearly all of the sacrificed individuals sampled were female – six out of seven – and none was genetically related to the tombs’ occupants.
Huangchengtai, located in the centre of Shimao and possibly the residence of the ruling class, also showed predominantly female sacrifices – 14 out of 19 – according to the paper.

Dong Yu, a researcher from the Institute of Cultural Heritage at Shandong University, and Ripan Malhi, an anthropologist at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, wrote an article about the findings in Nature. Neither was involved in the study itself.
They said the phenomenon of elite grave owners being buried alongside sacrificed female companions was observed thousands of years later in the Eastern Zhou period of the early Iron Age (770–221 BC), adding that it was unclear whether these were connected.
The study led by Fu and others also shed light on other major questions.
It revealed that the Shimao people descended from a population that had inhabited the region hundreds of years earlier and may have been connected to the Yangshao culture of the central plain or other cultures from the Ordos region.
The researchers also found that populations related to the Shimao culture received minor genetic influences from northern steppe regions and southern rice-farming cultures in the Yangtze River region.
Previous efforts to explain the emergence of hierarchically organised societies in East Asia focused on dispersed archaeological sites or early dynasties such as the Xia (2100-1600 BC) and Shang (1600-1046 BC).
The extensive archaeological record at Shimao “has further expanded our knowledge of early state-level communities”, the authors said.
In an indication of the study’s significance, the National Cultural Heritage Administration of China held a press conference on Thursday to present the findings.
The government bureau said it would support studies that could deepen the understanding of the origins of Chinese civilisation, while pledging to boost multidisciplinary collaboration and leverage scientific archaeology.

data.title: Hotels hit back as Tokyo plans to hike lodging tax with percentage-based system
data.description:
Tokyo’s hotel sector is fighting plans to replace the city’s long-standing flat-rate lodging tax with a percentage-based levy that could significantly increase costs for tourists and short-term visitors.
Analysts and industry groups argue the proposed three per cent tax unfairly targets overnight guests and could dampen Japan’s domestic travel market at a time when locals are already squeezed by a weak yen and rising living costs.
Under the current system introduced in 2002, travellers are charged a flat tax of 100 yen (US$0.63) per night if their room costs between 10,000 yen and 15,000 yen. The rate rises to 200 yen for rooms priced above that.
But Tokyo’s metropolitan government now wants to scrap that model in favour of a proportional system that reflects actual room rates – a move critics say disproportionately affects travellers staying at higher-end accommodation.
The revamped tax would also apply to “minpaku” – Japan’s term for Airbnb-style short-stay rentals – which were previously not subject to the hotel tax.


Masaharu Naruse, president of the Tokyo chapter of the Japan Hotel Association (JHA), warned the shift would place an unfair burden on lodging guests while ignoring other types of visitors who also use city services.
“A lodging tax is only levied on lodging guests among visitors to a region,” Naruse said in a statement issued before the latest reports on the proposal. “It inherently lacks fairness in comparison to visitors who do not stay overnight, representing a tax that strongly favours ‘taking from those who are easy to tax’. This approach must be handled with caution.”
According to national broadcaster NHK, the Tokyo government has been reviewing the lodging tax due to a sharp rise in tourist numbers, which has driven up the city’s administrative costs. Officials have opened the proposal to public comment but said they intend to implement the revised ordinance from April 2027.
In a position paper released in September, the JHA said the sector had delivered “significant economic benefits to the entire region” and warned that higher levies could undermine its contributions.
The group called for the current tax system to be maintained, citing four straight years of rising revenues including lodging tax receipts as evidence that “there is no recognised need to raise the tax rate”. It also argued that a percentage-based approach unfairly penalises guests at more expensive hotels.
Hotels contacted by This Week in Asia declined to comment on the proposal, but industry analysts pointed out that similar systems have been introduced in other major tourist hubs.
“This is standard practice in many other cities around the world and you could argue that a lot of inbound tourists do not even notice the tax on their bill,” said Ashley Harvey, a travel marketing analyst who has worked in Japan’s travel sector for more than 15 years.
“Foreign tourists are easy targets because anyone travelling all the way to Japan is not likely to change their mind over a couple of thousand yen, while it will have the support of many people in Tokyo as they do not have to pay it and, for the city, it will not lose them votes come election time,” he said.

Harvey said the rationale behind the policy was sound – visitors do use city services, from street lighting and policing to sanitation – but argued that officials would bolster their case by explaining how the tax revenue would be allocated.
Making clear whether the money is being used to upgrade services for residents or to ease their tax burden, he suggested, would help strengthen public support.
He noted that some cities choose to channel additional income from visitors back into the tourism sector, but argued this was unnecessary in a destination that already had “world-class” facilities for travellers such as Tokyo.
At the same time, he pointed out that the proposed tax risked inflicting further harm on Japan’s domestic travel industry.
“Given that close to 80 per cent of all tourism spend in Japan is domestic, any ‘tourism taxes’ will ultimately hurt Japanese citizens,” he said.
“Residents who can no longer afford to go abroad because the yen is so weak, wages are stagnant and costs at home are increasing – and now they are finding that tourism taxes are pricing them out of a domestic holiday, too.”

data.title: Congo and Rwanda presidents to meet in US to sign peace deal
data.description:
Congo’s President Felix Tshisekedi will travel to the US next week to sign a final agreement with his Rwandan counterpart aimed at securing peace in eastern Congo, the Congolese government said on Friday, capping months-long diplomatic efforts spearheaded by US President Donald Trump.
Congo’s government has often said its signing of the deal will be conditioned on Rwanda’s withdrawal of support for the M23 rebels in the conflict-battered east.
Tshisekedi’s confirmation of the December 4 signing comes a day after Rwanda’s President Paul Kagame expressed optimism over the deal, but cautioned that lasting peace in Congo’s eastern region could only be achieved if “people directly concerned are committed to achieve results”.

Eastern Congo has been battered by fighting between government forces and more than 100 armed groups, the most potent being the Rwanda-backed M23 rebels. The conflict escalated this year, with the M23 seizing the region’s main cities of Goma and Bukavu, worsening a humanitarian crisis that was already one of the world’s largest.
UN experts have said that between 3,000 and 4,000 Rwandan government forces are deployed in eastern Congo, operating alongside the M23. Rwanda denies such support but has said that any action taken in the conflict is to protect its territory.
In an interview on Friday, Tina Salama, a spokeswoman for Tshisekedi, said a peace deal must be reached with “no mixing or integration of M23 fighters”. She said the withdrawal of Rwandan troops had already been agreed to in an earlier agreement signed in June by both Congolese and Rwandan foreign ministers.

“We are seeking peace within the framework of regional integration,” Salama said. “What is non-negotiable for us is the territorial integrity of the DRC.”
Kagame on Thursday expressed concern that Congolese authorities were reneging on previous agreements and constantly shifting goalposts, adding that the success of the agreement would depend on the commitment of the two warring sides.
“Some of these processes will not work not just because we are meeting in Washington or powerful United States is involved, but until those people concerned directly are committed to achieve end results,” Kagame said.

data.title: US halts asylum cases after troop killing as Trump steps up migrant crackdown
data.description:
The United States is freezing all asylum decisions, officials said on Friday, as President Donald Trump hardens his anti-migrant stance after an Afghan national allegedly shot two National Guard members this week in Washington.
Wednesday’s attack on the soldiers – one of whom died from her injuries – has ignited a fresh crackdown on foreigners in the United States, with Trump also pledging to suspend migration from “third world countries”.
Joseph Edlow, director of the US Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), said his agency had “halted all asylum decisions until we can ensure that every alien is vetted and screened to the maximum degree possible”.
That followed Trump’s announcement late Thursday of plans to “permanently pause migration from all Third World Countries to allow the US system to fully recover”.
Asked which nationalities would be affected, the Department of Homeland Security pointed to a list of 19 countries – including Afghanistan, Cuba, Haiti, Iran and Myanmar – already facing US travel restrictions since June.
Meanwhile, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Friday that the US had temporarily stopped issuing visas to all individuals travelling on Afghan passports.
“The United States has no higher priority than protecting our nation and our people,” he said.

The shooting has brought together three politically explosive issues: Trump’s controversial use of the military on American soil, immigration and the lingering legacy of the 20-year conflict in Afghanistan.
Rahmanullah Lakanwal, 29, who allegedly opened fire on the guardsmen just a few blocks from the White House, had been part of a CIA-backed “partner force” fighting the Taliban in Afghanistan.
He entered the United States as part of a resettlement programme following the American military withdrawal from Afghanistan in 2021.
Jeanine Pirro, the US lawyer for Washington, said on Friday that Lakanwal would be charged with murder over the attack.
Sarah Beckstrom, a 20-year-old West Virginia National Guard member deployed in the US capital as part of what Trump called a crackdown on crime, died from her wounds.
The second injured soldier, 24-year-old Andrew Wolfe, was “fighting for his life”, Pirro told the Fox News programme Fox & Friends.
Attorney General Pam Bondi has pledged to seek the death penalty against Lakanwal, describing him as a “monster”.

In his social media post on Thursday, Trump also threatened to reverse “millions” of admissions granted under his predecessor Joe Biden, in a new escalation of his anti-immigration stance.
Separately, the USCIS said it would re-examine the green cards – permanent residency cards – issued to individuals who had migrated to the US from the same 19 countries also cited by the Department of Homeland Security.
More than 1.6 million green card holders, roughly 12 per cent of the total permanent resident population, were born in the countries listed, according to US immigration data.
Afghanistan has over 116,000 green card holders.
Shawn VanDiver, president of AfghanEvac, a group that helped resettle Afghans in the country after the military withdrawal, blasted Rubio’s move to halt all visa issuances.
“They are using a single violent individual as cover for a policy they have long planned,” he said in a statement.

Lakanwal had been living in the western state of Washington with his family and drove across the country to the capital before Wednesday’s shooting, officials said.
Trump has insisted that Lakanwal was granted unvetted access to the US because of lax asylum policies after the chaotic US withdrawal from Afghanistan under former president Biden.
However, AfghanEvac said the Afghans had undergone “some of the most extensive security vetting” of any migrants. It added that Lakanwal applied for asylum under Biden but received it later, under Trump.

data.title: Hongkongers mourn fire victims at citywide condolence points; toll at 128
data.description:
The three-day mourning period begins on Saturday.
Chief Executive John Lee, all principal government officials, members of the city’s top decision-making Executive Council and a group of civil servants observe a three-minute silence at 8am at the East Wing forecourt of the government headquarters to pay tribute to the victims of the fire.
It is also attended by Zhou Ji, director of Beijing’s liaison office.
All attendees, dressed in black, mourn the victims, with the national and Hong Kong flags flying at half-mast.

data.title: Difficult to say if fire-ravaged Hong Kong buildings can be fixed: experts
data.description:
The government should take the lead in assessing the structural safety of the estate engulfed by Hong Kong’s worst fire in decades before deciding whether it should be restored or redeveloped, experts have said, warning of liability problems and a long road ahead.
The future of Wang Fuk Court, where a massive fire raged through seven of its eight blocks for more than 43 hours before largely being put out, remains uncertain, with residents taking shelter or moving into temporary housing arranged by the authorities.
Specialists the Post talked to said either restoration or tearing down for redevelopment would be complicated.
“I believe the government should offer help in offering an assessment of the structural safety of the buildings … to offer flat owners a reliable and professional assessment on the building safety, the level of damage and to decide on whether the buildings are fit for renovation,” said Vincent Ho Kui-yip, president of the Hong Kong Institute of Building Safety.


While buildings’ structural safety could not be assessed at this point, Ho said he expected that the floor slabs of some flats could have been severely damaged by the prolonged fire.
He said concrete could expand and spall because of the heat and also under the rapid change in temperature following the use of water to put out the fire.
Reinforcement bars could also be distorted after exposure to high heat, he said.
“If there is no major damage in the overall structure of the buildings, it could take two years to replace all the facilities, as well as outer walls and windows,” Ho said.
It would not be possible to make an estimate on the cost as the actual severity level of damage remained unknown, he said.




Structural engineer Ngai Hok-yan said it could take around six to seven months to conduct a thorough inspection of the structural safety of all seven buildings.
“Every part of the buildings will need to be inspected,” Ngai said. “Inspection of damage on the outer walls will also be needed, and it could take a long time.”
Even if renovations were possible, all the lift systems would need to be replaced, together with electrical wiring and gas and water pipes, he said.
The issue could be further complicated if rebuilding was needed, and it could take at least four years to complete, Ho said.
Factors that would need to be considered included whether the foundation could be kept and whether the design of the buildings would need to be changed to comply with new standards.
“Even if the foundations can be kept, reinforcement will be needed, as the standards for wind load and bearing capacity set more than 40 years ago will be different now,” Ho said.
“There are also new requirements in rebar specifications compared with some 40 years ago.”
Building designs would also need to be updated to meet the latest standards and requirements if redevelopment went ahead, he said.

Apart from technical issues, issues in liability would need to be sorted out to proceed with any decisions on restoration or redevelopment, according to lawmaker Andrew Lam Siu-lo, a veteran town planner.
Lam said the ownership rights of outer walls and public areas of the estate were unclear, along with the property rights of flat owners.
He also pointed to liability issues if some flat owners of Wang Fuk Court, an estate under the Home Ownership Scheme (HOS), had not paid a premium to the government and did not have full ownership of their property.
“If the flat owners unfortunately died [in the fire], how should we handle the ownership?” he said. “Who will make decisions on their behalf if any redevelopment plan is involved?”
Liabilities of the fire had yet to be ascertained, and it also remained unclear how much of the losses could be covered by insurance, Lam said.
“Flat owners are victims but also responsible persons. How do you count the costs? No one knows,” he said.
Click here for the full list of candidates.

data.title: Malaysia’s Sabah begins voting as Anwar faces test over coalition’s performance
data.description:
Voters in Sabah began casting their ballots on Saturday, in a state election seen as a referendum on Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s leadership midway into his first term.
Anwar, who came to office in 2022 after pulling together a rag-tag coalition of former political rivals, depends on Sabah to prop up his parliamentary supermajority. His coalition has so far fended off the challenge of a Malay-nationalist opposition bloc and pushed through unpopular measures, including subsidy cuts to pare down government debt.
But in Sabah – where 596 candidates are contesting 73 seats – the prime minister faces a young electorate tired of failed promises by the central government over the decades to provide a reliable water and electricity supply, while taking the profits of the state’s natural resources to develop the peninsula.
Among the nearly 1.8 million voters – over half of whom are aged between 18 and 39 – many are angry with members of the dominant political dynasties in Sabah, who critics accuse of being mired in corruption scandals and have benefited the most from the exploitation of the state’s resources, including oil and gas, forests and marine life.
“The main issue is accountability, not more promises,” said Sudirman Arshad, 22, a student activist from the town of Lahad Datu in Sabah’s impoverished east coast district.
While it will not affect the makeup of the federal parliament, analysts see the state election as a key indicator of Anwar’s popularity outside his strongholds in the peninsula as he plots a path towards the general election due by early 2028.
Anwar will be 80 by then, but political observers believe he may run for re-election.

The prime minister needed incumbent state ruling party Gagasan Rakyat Sabah (GRS) to perform well at the election on Saturday or risk triggering a domino effect in polls in other states over the next two years, analyst Adib Zalkapli, told This Week in Asia.
“The best case scenario for Anwar is a GRS-dominated state government with PH playing the role as a minor partner in a [state] coalition government,” Adib said, referring to Anwar’s Pakatan Harapan federal alliance.
But a return of Warisan, the former ruling party in Sabah, to power could spell trouble for Anwar and raise questions about his political survival over the remainder of his term.
“This will [prematurely] force the federal government to get into general election mode full time,” said Adib, managing director at geopolitical and public affairs firm Viewfinder Global Affairs.
The morning rain did not stop queues forming early outside polling centres across the state, well before doors opened at 7.30am to mark the start of the one-day voting process. Among the election candidates seen casting their vote early was Warisan’s President Shafie Apdal.
The Election Commission (EC) on Friday said it was aiming for a voter turnout of more than 60 per cent, despite predictions of poor weather potentially causing road congestion over the weekend.

“Everyone should have the opportunity to exercise the right to vote,” EC chairman Ramlan Harun told reporters during a check on a polling centre in the outskirts of Sabah’s capital, Kota Kinabalu.
Ahead of the election, over two weeks of campaigning, Anwar focused on addressing long-standing complaints, such as poor basic infrastructure and low wages amid soaring inflation.
He has pledged 1 billion ringgit (US$241 million) in federal funds to solve persistent water shortages in Sabah by next year.
His administration faces additional pressure from voters to uphold a court ruling in October recognising Sabah’s constitutional right to 40 per cent of annual federal revenue collected from the state.
The revenue entitlement is a key campaign pledge of local parties, especially Warisan, which analysts widely expect to be the best performer in the election, albeit short of a simple majority of 37 seats needed to claim power.
At the campaign finale for his coalition on Thursday night, Anwar gave his assurance that the federal government would abide by the court’s decision.

“Forty per cent is no longer an issue. It will be implemented. [By] which government? The current [federal] government … this is what we have to do,” he told the thousands gathered for the rally in Kota Kinabalu.
PH has also been tarred by a mining-related corruption scandal, with bribery charges filed against two GRS lawmakers.
Anwar’s senior political secretary resigned on Tuesday, just hours after allegations surfaced that he had taken over 600,000 ringgit (US$144,600) in gifts and bribes from a businessman tied to the scandal.
Shamsul Iskandar Mohd Akin said the move was to “defend myself” against attacks that could “harm the image of the government”.

data.title: Work in progress: Hong Kong reaps the fruits of fintech seeds sown nearly 10 years ago
data.description:
When Alvin Kwock, co-founder of digital insurer OneDegree, took part in the inaugural Hong Kong FinTech Week in November 2016, it was held at a venue suitable for only a few hundred people.
“As it was the first ever fintech event in Hong Kong, only fintech start-ups were interested, no big financial institutions paid attention,” Kwock said. “People did not have a clear idea of fintech as it was still in an exploratory stage.”
In the years since, fintech in Hong Kong has gone through a sea change. “Now fintech is no longer confined to a few start-ups; it’s on the top of the agenda of banks and insurers,” Kwock said.
The venue has moved from the PMQ (formerly the Police Married Quarters) to the massive Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre, with the number of attendees growing 18-fold to 45,000 people. At the recent forum, speakers included some of the most high-profile figures in the financial sector like HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery and Standard Chartered Bank CEO Bill Winters.

This maturation has intensified Hong Kong’s long-standing rivalry with Singapore. The two cities are competing to develop fintech, a rivalry highlighted this month as both hosted their 10th annual flagship fintech festivals.
While attendance in Hong Kong may have been thinner than in Singapore, the city has an edge over its Southeast Asian rival, thanks to its vibrant capital market providing funding for fintech companies and serving as a gateway to the Greater Bay Area.
Hong Kong secured the world’s top fintech ranking for the first time in the Global Financial Centres Index in September, while Singapore ranked fourth, according to the report by Z/Yen and the City of London that tracked 116 centres based on criteria like regulations, access to finance and talent.
In the inaugural survey on fintech in 2017, Hong Kong ranked seventh and Singapore eighth. The same year, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) introduced the first phase of fintech development.


Since then, the HKMA has issued eight digital banking licences, with the lenders offering online-only services. This week, British digital bank Revolut announced plans to launch operations in Hong Kong.
The arrival of global players is a direct result of a concerted regulatory push – the HKMA’s fintech initiatives, the Insurance Authority’s promotion of virtual insurance, and the Securities and Futures Commission’s rules for virtual asset trading. Bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing’s listing reforms too have paved the way for start-ups to raise funds.
“Hong Kong’s fintech development in the past decade has been nothing short of transformative,” said HKMA chief executive Eddie Yue Wai-man in a written interview. The 18-fold increase in the number of participants in this year’s FinTech Week compared with nine years ago was “proof not only of growing interest but also of a hub that has become a magnet for talent, ideas and ambition in fintech”, he said.
However, a broader measure of digital competitiveness reveals ongoing challenges. Despite Hong Kong’s many advantages, Singapore ranked third and Hong Kong fourth in the 2025 World Digital Competitiveness Ranking released earlier this month.


The report, however, noted that Hong Kong performed the worst in “IT integration”, placing 29th overall, due to low scores in government cybersecurity capacity and legal framework for privacy protection.
Singapore, which topped the ranking in 2024, fell behind Switzerland and the US this year.
The report said Singapore was strong in its digital regulatory framework and hi-tech patent grants, topping these categories. The institute also highlighted Singapore’s weakness in two areas, where it ranked 29th in technology, media and telecommunications stock market capitalisation and 61st in telecommunications investment.
Analysts and fintech firms regard Singapore and Hong Kong as Asian fintech leaders, albeit with different roles.
“Singapore offers a more accessible pathway for regional expansion across Asean, whereas Hong Kong’s competitive strength remains closely tied to the China-Hong Kong corridor and its leadership in regulated digital-asset innovation,” said Benjamin Quinlan, CEO and managing partner of Quinlan & Associates, a Hong Kong-based consultancy.
Quinlan said Hong Kong had also made progress in digital asset and tokenisation initiatives, including the coming stablecoin regime and tokenised government bond issuance, which provided a unique competitive angle in this emerging sector.


Singapore, by contrast, had positioned itself as a launch pad for the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, while the country had a clean regulatory environment and was innovation-friendly, supported by comparatively lower barriers to entry and a strong investor appetite, Quinlan said.
The HKMA’s Yue said the gateway role to mainland China would continue to be a major advantage for Hong Kong’s fintech development, as companies took advantage of cross-border trading schemes in stocks, bonds, swaps, exchange-traded products and wealth management.
Yue added that the development of fintech had benefited many sectors like digital banks, while also making it easier for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) to get bank loans via the Commercial Data Interchange (CDI).
The CDI, which was launched in October 2022 as part of the HKMA’s Fintech 2025 strategy, is a financial data platform designed to make it easier for businesses, especially SMEs, to access financial services. The CDI had facilitated over 71,000 loan applications totalling HK$58.1 billion (US$7.5 billion) for SMEs until the end of September.
“Think of a wonton noodle shop that could use the CDI to share its historical turnover data from point-of-sale terminals with a bank, which could then use this alternative data for a more accurate credit assessment and loan approval, all without requiring traditional collateral,” Yue said. “We are also advancing trade finance digitalisation through CargoX to support SME traders in a complex global trade landscape.”
Project CargoX is a public-private collaboration led by the HKMA to use cargo and trade data to improve trade finance.

One of the HKMA’s first fintech initiatives was the launch of the Faster Payment System in 2018, with the platform currently boasting more than 18 million users. This was followed by licences for eight digital banks in 2019.
Even in a market with more than 150 lenders, these virtual banks had 3.4 million customers, with HK$77 billion in deposits and HK$29 billion in loans as of June, according to the HKMA.
To build on this decade of progress and solidify Hong Kong’s future position, Yue unveiled the Fintech 2030 road map this month. The five-year programme will launch more than 40 initiatives in four areas: data, artificial intelligence, resilience and tokenisation.
The city has also incubated some prominent fintech unicorns – start-ups with a valuation of at least US$1 billion – like Airwallex, HashKey Group, WeLab, Micro Connect and ZA Group.
Hong Kong currently has more than 1,200 fintech companies, a 10 per cent increase from last year, with the sector’s revenue expected to exceed US$606 billion by 2032, according to a recent report by the Financial Services Development Council.
The UK’s Revolut will add to the tally.
Revolut is a global disrupter with over 65 million clients and has a strong presence in the US, Europe and Asia, so adding Hong Kong was the next stop in the expansion, said Camilo Ramirez, head of financial services at Sia Partners Hong Kong, a consultancy.
“While Hong Kong looks like a saturated market, it still lags behind in digital capabilities compared with Europe and mainland China,” Ramirez said. “With the HKMA launching the fintech road map, Revolut would land in a perfect environment to bring its innovative capabilities to Hong Kong at a pivotal and exciting moment for the financial services industry.”

One Degree’s Kwock said the conversations around fintech had continued to evolve at FinTech Week. Initially, it was about exploring the use of fintech, which later shifted to digital banks, digital insurance and virtual assets trading, before moving to AI this year, he said.
“Cybersecurity will be a key issue going forward as the city becomes a virtual-asset trading hub,” Kwock said.
Meanwhile, home-grown fintechs like WeLab have been using their early-mover advantage to good use by expanding in Southeast Asia.
WeLab serves 70 million customers via its WeLend online lending platform and two digital banks in Hong Kong and Indonesia, WeLab Bank and Bank Saqu, respectively.
“Positioned at the nexus of two major growth corridors – northbound to the Greater Bay Area and mainland China, and southbound to the rapidly growing and digitally transformative markets of Southeast Asia, Hong Kong acts as a natural springboard for regional fintech expansion,” said Simon Loong, founder and group CEO of WeLab.
Fintech has also transformed one of Hong Kong’s oldest banks. The 160-year-old HSBC has seen an increase in digital use across all banking transactions.
“We are already a fully digital bank, prioritising digital channels to deliver daily banking services and engage with our customers,” said Maggie Ng, CEO and head of retail banking and wealth at HSBC Hong Kong.
Ng said HSBC would take part in the HKMA’s Fintech 2030 projects. The bank had already introduced tokenised deposits for customers, and its digital asset platform, HSBC Orion, was used to launch the world’s largest digital bond issuance by the Hong Kong government this month, she said.
The bank recently launched HSBC Gold Token, a tokenised gold product for retail customers in Hong Kong.
Also this month, Hong Kong and Shenzhen launched a joint fintech action plan to promote cross-boundary financial innovation, setting a target of establishing over 20 fintech application scenarios by the end of 2027. Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu, who signed the initial pact with Shenzhen, said the two sides would implement various initiatives to facilitate high-quality fintech development.
Paul Sin, chair of the web3 and technology committee for Greater China at CPA Australia, an accounting industry body, said Hong Kong’s Fintech 2030 initiative provided a clear road map for the next five years.
One element that Hong Kong needed to develop was a decentralised identity (DID) system, which would allow financial firms to easily verify users’ identities.
“Only when firms can easily verify clients’ identities using technology like DID can fintech solutions be both innovative and compliant,” Sin said.

data.title: Shanghai bets on manufacturing to buttress finance in two-pronged growth plan
data.description:
Shanghai, mainland China’s top finance and trade hub, is set to double down on efforts to prevent the “hollowing out” of its manufacturing sector even as it pursues global financial centre status.
The city’s next five-year plan, which will cover the period from 2026 to 2030, is expected to contain measures designed to lift manufacturing’s share of gross domestic product to no less than 25 per cent, in line with a national imperative to bolster the real economy and promote cutting-edge technology.
The move would make Shanghai one of the first major Chinese cities to include a specific goal for stabilising the manufacturing sector in its next five-year plan, and would run concurrently with a broader campaign to win the city recognition as a fully-fledged global financial centre by 2035.
The latter goal was reaffirmed in a document released in June by the Communist Party’s Central Financial Commission, a party organ headed by Premier Li Qiang that oversees China’s vast financial holdings.
“Shanghai officials are resolved to avoid the fate of economic hollowing out and the exodus of industrial enterprises, a phenomenon prevalent among some global cities as they develop into international financial hubs,” said Fu Weigang, president of the Shanghai Institute of Finance and Law think tank.
“Shanghai aims to have two legs of balanced growth: a competitive services sector like finance plus advanced manufacturing, to make the economy more resilient.
“It’s more like learning from the lessons of Hong Kong, New York and others.”


Shanghai’s 25 per cent goal, first broached for discussion in official policy papers in 2016, was also reiterated in a three-year action plan gazetted last year on the building of a complete and modern manufacturing ecosystem – something lacking in Hong Kong and New York.
China’s top leadership has also pledged to prevent industrial hollowing out by putting a floor under manufacturing’s declining share of economic output.
But in an outline of official priorities for the next five years, which offered a detailed interpretation of proposals for the next national five-year plan, top policymakers admitted it was a trend that was difficult to reverse.
The Communist Party’s Central Committee unveiled its proposals for the 2026-2030 blueprint last month, with a focus on building a modern industrial system by “maintaining a reasonable share of manufacturing”, though it did not set a specific target.
For Shanghai, keeping its factories humming and upgrading production lines remains a tall order.
China’s largest city economy, with GDP hitting 5.39 trillion yuan (US$761 billion) last year, up 5 per cent year on year, is seeing its booming financial sector crowd out industrial producers.
The service sector accounted for 79.1 per cent of the city’s economy in the first half of this year, up from 78.2 per cent last year and an all-time high, leaving the share of manufacturing at around 20 per cent given the city’s negligible agricultural output.
In the first three quarters of this year, Shanghai’s industrial output grew by 3.9 per cent year on year, but that was outpaced by the local economy’s 5.5 per cent overall growth.
China’s national push for tech and economic security for the next five years hinges on key cities like Shanghai
Wang Sizheng, Shanghai Institute of Macroeconomics
But Fu emphasised that manufacturing spanned a wide spectrum in Shanghai, adding the city’s economy was still more diversified and balanced than that of many other metropolises: “Shanghai’s rise was fuelled by its industrial base … Does New York City have a big auto sector? No. Does London have a huge shipbuilding yard or a big refinery? No. Does Hong Kong have an aircraft maker or a big chip foundry? No.”
Automobiles, shipbuilding, petrochemicals, civil aviation, chipmaking and integrated circuits are being touted as the pillars of Shanghai’s manufacturing upgrade. Tesla operates a sprawling factory in Shanghai, and the Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China – maker of the country’s indigenous C919 passenger airliner – and chipmaker SMIC are both headquartered in the city.
Fu said that in contrast to cities that had failed to avert deindustrialisation, Shanghai had an ample supply of land for new industries and also benefited from the government’s iron resolve and systematic planning.
Wang Sizheng, president of the Shanghai Institute of Macroeconomics, told Jiefang Daily, the news organ of the city’s party committee, last month that defending Shanghai’s manufacturing base carried nationwide significance.
“China’s national push for tech and economic security for the next five years hinges on key cities like Shanghai to be on the vanguard of propping up manufacturing,” he said.
Wang, who also sits on a panel advising on the drafting of Shanghai’s new five-year plan, revealed the city would formulate ways to juggle the tasks of nurturing its financial sector while retaining and cultivating industrial enterprises and clusters.
“It’s not just about Shanghai, but rather the city’s responsibility to take the lead nationwide in promoting manufacturing, represent China in global competition and safeguard national industrial security,” he added.

data.title: Why Chinese women like me are done being quiet and ‘appropriate’
data.description:
In China, girls are taught early to be “appropriate” – to listen, not lead; to speak gently, not loudly. We grow up believing that quietness is likeability, and likeability is safety. But something is shifting.
When Chinese writer Jiang Fangzhou said to me during a recent interview, “I can’t be waiting for some influential man to say, ‘I’ll give you an hour to talk about literature’. You can’t be waiting for a seat, for a microphone”, it struck a chord far beyond us. It was a generational declaration – for women who are done being echoes.
Jiang’s trajectory is familiar to many in China. She began writing at seven, published her first book at nine and rose to fame as a newspaper columnist at 12. Once celebrated as a literary prodigy, she was later consumed by controversy, drawing plaudits and attacks in equal measure.
When I first interviewed her a decade ago, she was visibly weary of being labelled a “beautiful female writer”. “I still want to write a satisfying long work,” she said then. “But if I never do, being a ‘young writer’, a ‘middle-aged writer’ and one day, an ‘old writer’ is fine too.”
Back then, few of us – myself included – had the language to talk about gender or identity. Feminism felt abstract, even Western. We didn’t yet see how deeply the idea of female “appropriateness” shaped our behaviour. I mistook restraint for maturity and believed that to be articulate was to risk being disliked.
So we adapted. We learned the rules, fit in and rarely questioned the rules themselves.

After several quiet years away from the internet, Jiang re-emerged this year not as a former prodigy, but as a creator consciously reclaiming her voice. With her new literary podcast, she prepares 10,000-character scripts for every episode, sharing the joy of reading and her own reflections. She has taken back the microphone.
Jiang often cites A Room of One’s Own, the essay in which Virginia Woolf advised that a woman must have five hundred pounds a year and a room of her own to write.
Jiang takes the idea further: “Perhaps a woman shouldn’t just have a room of her own, she should have a house of her own. With a bedroom, a study and, more importantly, a living room – a space for discourse she hosts.”
In our Confucian-rooted patriarchal culture, women are praised for gentleness, not for volume. We learn to nod at the right moment, to speak softly, to never interrupt. Our tone must please; our logic must bend; even our anger must be graceful.
This isn’t weakness – it’s conditioning. From childhood, we’re rewarded for being “understanding”, “cooperative”, “easy to work with”. We internalise those expectations until self-censorship feels like common sense. In meetings, we preface disagreement with polite disclaimers. In social situations, we smooth our edges for harmony.

Over time, we become fluent in quietness. Our voices shrink, our faces blur and our opinions dissolve into politeness.
This obedience isn’t just personal – it’s systemic. It lives in the way teachers praise “sensible girls”, how offices value “team players” and how public opinion punishes women deemed “difficult”. The control imposed on women isn’t loud or violent; it’s subtle and internalised. For years, I believed that to be heard, one had to be agreeable. But silence doesn’t earn respect; it only erases you.
Ten years after our first meeting, when I sat down with Jiang again last month, the atmosphere felt entirely different. Our planned 30-minute interview stretched to 90 minutes; we talked until the camera battery died and day turned to dusk.
She spoke with excitement about how female creators today “finally feel ready to attract their own audiences”. Then she turned to me: “So you should start a podcast too! Did I inspire you?”
Even after the camera stopped rolling, she kept brainstorming: “Make something you love! Maybe a nonfiction series, or reflections on your years studying overseas. Or if that feels too heavy and sensitive – talk about parenting!”

A few days later, she sent me an email: “Looking forward to seeing you again – and I hope you start your podcast soon. I think you’ll be great at it.”
That small message meant more than encouragement. It reflected a quiet cultural shift. Ten years ago, Chinese women often saw each other as rivals; mutual support was rare. Today, even though opportunities are scarcer, competition harsher, and paths narrower, more women are not only speaking up but also passing the microphone along.
“Girls help girls” has become more than a hashtag – it’s a social current born not of abundance, but of scarcity. In a tighter, more unforgiving landscape, solidarity itself becomes an act of defiance.
Since the #MeToo movement, Chinese women have become bolder in public conversation. Across generations, they are speaking openly about marriage, motherhood, workplace bias and emotional freedom. Podcasts, social media and women-led communities have created new spaces for expression and connection.
Woolf once urged women to “write what you wish to write; that is all that matters”. A century later, a new generation of Chinese women are doing exactly that – speaking out, building their own living rooms and refusing to be well-behaved echoes any longer. Perhaps this is the most powerful response to “involution” yet: when crowded race tracks offer little room for growth, women are carving out new squares and inviting each other in.
